# Questions in Livestream 47

Live Stream #47: Passover 2022.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=293" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:53</a> | What is the numerical meaning of 666? | He surveys major views, noting gematria is common, but also entertains an Old Testament allusion to Solomon's wealth. | He says the dominant scholarly view is gematria for Nero Caesar, but he is not satisfied that this exhausts the symbolism. He notes that the text uses Greek letters rather than modern Arabic numerals, then points to Solomon receiving 666 talents of gold as an Old Testament archetype of rapid wealth, power, and control. His own leaning is that Revelation may allude both to Nero and to Solomon-like Old Testament symbolism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:03</a> | Is there linguistic reason to treat some monotheistic-sounding passages as ANE hyperbole? | He says Isaiah's language is better read as genuine monotheism, not mere hyperbole masking polytheism. | He argues that Isaiah 44-48 repeats claims such as God being first and last with no other god too persistently to treat them as mere ancient Near Eastern royal hyperbole. He distinguishes older Middle Bronze and Late Bronze hyperbolic language from Iron Age Israel's prophetic context, where he thinks the text is pressing full monotheism rather than hiding henotheism or polytheism under exaggerated phrasing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=800" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:20</a> | What position do you take on henotheism, monotheism, and monolatry in the Bible? | He argues the biblical materials lean toward monolatry grounded in a broader monotheistic outlook. | He cautions that terms like henotheism, monotheism, and monolatry are modern categories and are not always mutually exclusive in ancient religion. For the biblical texts, he sees clear Torah passages calling Israel to serve one God only, and he thinks that monolatry quickly collapses toward monarchial monotheism because Yahweh is portrayed as transcendent. Divine-council texts do not persuade him that the Bible assumes equal polytheistic gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=1160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:20</a> | How could God take Enoch if Enoch is understood as a clan or progenitor figure? | He suggests Enoch may be the prominent end figure of a clan line rather than only its biological founder. | He explains that a progenitor can be either the founder of a clan or the most famous figure at the end of a clan line who becomes credited with the whole group. On that reading, Enoch's clan could have begun when he was 65 and continued for about 300 years, while the remembered Enoch was the climactic figure whose being taken by God made him the name-bearing ancestor of the line. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=1285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:25</a> | In Genesis 47:7-9, was Jacob really 130 or 131? | Scholars debate it; he leans toward a missing ancestor or clan-age explanation, while noting an honorary or exaggerated age is also possible. | He says scholars debate whether Jacob's stated age is literal, clan-based, or honorific. Many would read the years as representing a clan line rather than one biological life, possibly with an unnamed ancestor included. He also allows that the age could function symbolically or honorifically, comparing Joseph's 110 years to an Egyptian ideal age for a sage, but he personally leans toward a missing-ancestor explanation while admitting the evidence is not clear-cut. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=1599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:39</a> | Is man body and soul, or body, soul, and spirit? | He says the issue is debated, but he leans somewhat toward a tripartite model. | He says he is almost leaning tripartite, not mainly because of one prooftext but because of metaphysical considerations. Genesis 2 shows the body animated by God's breath so that the person becomes a living soul, and he treats the soul as the person rather than merely the body. He still frames the answer as tentative because bipartite and tripartite readings depend heavily on broader theological and philosophical assumptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=1982" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:02</a> | Could Ezekiel's Magog battle fit events around 1071-1097 under a preterist reading? | He says the parallels do not convince him and that the proposal does not really solve the passage. | He is not persuaded by preterism here and says it has serious problems with apocalyptic literature, including how apocalyptic texts are recontextualized across generations. The proposed identification of Magog with events around 1071-1097 does not fit well for him, partly because Israel's status in the land and the final-judgment framing do not line up. He also objects that some preterist readings require an early date for Revelation that he thinks conflicts with church history and textual clues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=2467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:07</a> | Was Deuteronomy a Josianic forgery according to the documentary hypothesis? | He is unconvinced, arguing the hypothesis lacks internal consistency in how its advocates divide the Pentateuch. | He does not think Deuteronomy should be called a forgery, noting that its legal form has ancient Near Eastern parallels and may allow for edits or expansions without proving fraud. His main criticism is that documentary-hypothesis advocates often disagree sharply over which texts belong to which source or redactor, leaving only a broad claim that Deuteronomy is late while lacking a stable, testable division of the Pentateuch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=2740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:40</a> | When was the Last Supper relative to Passover, given John's chronology? | He works through the preparation-day question and treats the timing problem as real but explainable within the Gospel data. | He reads the Last Supper as taking place on Thursday night before the Sabbath, with the crucifixion on Friday and the resurrection on Sunday. He notes that the Gospels' concern to remove Jesus' body before the Sabbath supports this sequence, and he warns that the issue is complicated by Jewish day reckoning and by Greek prepositions, especially genitive and dative constructions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=3022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:22</a> | Does Satan have any connection with Egyptian myth? | He says the biblical term and role develop in their own context and should not be simply collapsed into Egyptian mythic figures. | He explains that `ha-satan` functions as a title or job description meaning the accuser, especially in Job. Egyptian myth has malevolent elements, but Egyptian deities are often ambivalent rather than divided into simple good and evil figures. Later portrayals of Seth become more hostile, but he rejects attempts to derive the biblical Satan from Seth or Sutek, calling Seth a storm deity of unbridled power rather than Satan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=3456" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:36</a> | Do you think Judas Iscariot was a zealot? | The name Iscariot is suggestive and the idea is reasonable, but he has not studied it closely enough to be certain. | He says the name Iscariot is suggestive and that a zealot connection makes sense on the surface, noting that some scholars have proposed it. He refuses to state it as certain because New Testament studies are outside his main field and he has not checked whether the proposal has been refuted or remains viable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=3734" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:14</a> | What part of the Holy Scriptures caused you more personal conflict? | Hebrews troubles him most because of its warnings about apostasy and people thinking they are in the faith when they are not. | He says Joshua no longer creates much personal conflict for him because he thinks he understands the conquest rhetoric, but Hebrews terrifies him precisely because he understands its warnings. He describes Hebrews as oscillating between Christ's supremacy and severe warnings about apostasy, especially people who believe they are in the faith but drift or turn away. For him, Romans is the carrot and Hebrews is the stick. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=4112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:32</a> | Besides Pharaoh's dynasty, were there long-standing important noble families or dynasties in Egypt? | Yes; provincial noble families and nomarch lines could last for centuries and formed major power bases. | He says Egypt had many noble families, only some of which became royal. The Theban royal family was especially long-lasting, but each province also had hereditary nomarchs or governors, and these provincial houses formed Egypt's political power base. Kings maintained relationships with such families, including marriage alliances, and some nomarch lines endured for centuries even when they lost royal status. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=4285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:25</a> | If the Mount Ebal curse amulet is genuine, does it challenge the late Exodus date? | No; he says the archaeological context fits a later setting, and early-date claims rely too heavily on subjective paleography. | He says the amulet came from a fill context associated with the Late Bronze IIB to Iron I transition, which does not threaten a late Exodus model. His objection is that early-date advocates are privileging paleographic claims over the archaeological context, even though verified early Hebrew paleography is too sparse to carry that weight. He also says lead isotope data only broadly indicates a Late Bronze range, not the specific early date being claimed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=5358" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:18</a> | How should we assess the claim that a king of Sumer was Kushite because Kush in Egyptian and Sumerian are the same without vowels? | He rejects it as a faulty etymology; cuneiform signs can have multiple phonemes, and the comparison does not work. | He says the argument misunderstands ancient languages and likely confuses `kush` with a Sumerian value closer to `gish`. Because Mesopotamian writing uses graphemes that can represent multiple phonemes, superficial consonant comparisons can produce false etymologies. He treats the claim as linguistically unreliable and not a serious basis for identifying a Sumerian king as Kushite. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=5592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:12</a> | How was Messiah in the tomb for three days and nights from Friday to Sunday? | Jewish inclusive reckoning counts partial days; Friday before sunset, Sabbath, and Sunday morning can be counted as into the third day. | He says the phrase should not be counted as three complete modern days and nights. In Jewish sunset-to-sunset reckoning, Friday before sunset counts as day one, the Sabbath counts as the second day, and Sunday morning is into the third day. He summarizes the principle as part of a day counting as a day, just as part of a year can count as a year. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=5950" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:10</a> | Are Genesis 1 and 2 evidence of J and P plus a sloppy redactor, and is their similarity to ANE texts usually held by the same scholars? | It depends on the scholar; Genesis interacts with ANE material but is not simply copying it, and the documentary hypothesis does not solve the issue. | He says source assignments for Genesis 1-2 vary widely by scholar, with different criteria producing different divisions. He agrees Genesis is interacting with ancient Near Eastern creation traditions, including Mesopotamian and Egyptian motifs, but he stresses that interaction is not copying because the wording and phrasing are not close. In his view, intertextual engagement gives Genesis context, while late source theories can actually complicate claims that it directly copied much older material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=6335" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:35</a> | Do you have recommendations for resources on Gnosticism? | He cannot strongly recommend a general book; he mentions his own work but says many available resources have serious problems. | He mentions that his own master's thesis was published on Egyptian origins of Gnosticism, but says it is expensive and limited in scope. He discusses Edwin Yamauchi respectfully but thinks Yamauchi's engagement with Bultmann still leaves problems, including too-late an emergence for Gnosticism. He says many resources are written either by New Testament scholars who only dabble in Gnosticism or by specialists committed to many-gospels theories he rejects, so he will not fully endorse a general book. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=6596" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:56</a> | Are there suggested books or places to learn Hebrew wordplay outside a university or seminary? | He knows no single collected resource and usually looks to commentaries; biblical names often carry wordplay. | He says he does not know a good collected book on Hebrew wordplay offhand. He points instead to commentaries and gives examples from names: Nabal meaning fool and names in Ruth functioning as meaningful, didactic wordplays rather than ordinary parental choices. His practical answer is that wordplay is common, especially in names, but he usually finds it case by case in commentaries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=6818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:38</a> | What are your thoughts about how widespread using the Lord's name in vain has become? | The main issue is bearing God's name lightly by misrepresenting him; expletive use is only a smaller part of the commandment. | He says using God's name as an expletive is a real but minor part of the command. The major issue is taking God's name onto oneself while living in a way that misrepresents him, like a franchisee damaging the name of the one they represent. He also adds that the command opposes attempts to manipulate God by invoking his name, drawing a contrast with magical uses of divine names in surrounding cultures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=7380" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:00</a> | What did you mean about having seen things that make atheism unlikely for you? | He says paranormal or demonic encounters make becoming an atheist very difficult for him. | He says he has had paranormal encounters and experiences with spirits, including phenomena he regards as demonic and malevolent. He emphasizes that he is not attributing this to drugs or alcohol and says such things became more concrete as he engaged the topic more directly. Because of those experiences, he says it would now be very difficult for him to become an atheist, though he declines to elaborate further. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=7566" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:06</a> | Is it fair to criticize there being no evidence for Israelites in Egypt if "Israel" starts only with Saul? | He says no; Israelite identity predates the monarchy, so that objection is not persuasive. | He says Israel did not begin with Saul, since Egyptian evidence already refers to Israel around the time of Merneptah, long before the monarchy. He clarifies that in Egypt the group would not yet be called Israelites in the later national sense, but sons of Jacob or Israel, Levantine Semites, Asiatics, and a mixed multitude. In his model, Israelite identity proper forms in the Sinai before the Merneptah reference, not with Saul. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=7761" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:21</a> | Could you explain Jonah's Sheol language and its relation to Christ? | He treats Jonah 2 as a strong typological text that helps explain Christ's use of Jonah imagery. | He reads Jonah 2 as death language rather than merely survival language: Jonah calls from Sheol, descends beneath the roots of the mountains, and is brought up from the pit. Falk says this makes Jonah a stronger type of Christ because the sign of Jonah is not simply being hidden in a fish but passing through death and deliverance after three days. He criticizes depictions that treat Jonah as merely sitting comfortably alive inside a whale. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=8260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:40</a> | Is Goliath's height of six cubits and a span meant literally? | He sees no major problem with treating the giant stature as literal. | He says six cubits and a span, roughly nine feet, does not trouble him as a literal claim. He notes that Egyptian satire texts refer to Shasu as six cubits tall and that modern human height already shows large variation, though he would like skeletal evidence for such tall Philistines or Canaanites. He thinks such height would be rare and impractical, but also useful as intimidation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=8456" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:56</a> | Any updates regarding the Mount Ebal amulet? | No decisive updates; scans and RTI images are not published, latest images are not compelling, and only a couple of letters seem clear. | He says Galil has published something new, but he has not read it and has heard it is not good. The key problem remains that tomographic scans and RTI images have not been released, while the images he has seen are not compelling. He can make out only an aleph and a resh clearly, and he suspects it may be a curse tablet because lead tablets often are, not because the proposed full inscription has been demonstrated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=8719" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:19</a> | Would Manfred Bietak agree that Israelites were at Avaris until the Ramesside dynasty? | He says Bietak's view shifted over time and that today he would likely accept at least a Ramesside or 19th-dynasty possibility. | He says Bietak's answer depends on the stage of his career because his interpretation of Avaris changed as excavations continued. Falk describes Bietak moving from a memorial view tied to Hyksos memory, toward recognizing Semitic Asiatics at Avaris into the Ramesside period, and then to a later Exodus possibility. He says the evidence of civilian occupation shifting to cemetery use in the reign of Ramesses II points especially toward a mid-19th-dynasty setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=9439" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:19</a> | Is Easter derived from Ashtar, Ishtar, or Isis? | No; he treats that as false etymology and says the Christian date is tied to Passover and lunar calculations, not those deities. | He rejects the Ashtar, Ishtar, and Isis derivations as debunked false etymologies based on superficial sound similarity. He notes that New Testament references are to Passover or Pesach, and that Easter's Christian calculation developed through lunar tables and festival reckoning. He describes it as tied to Passover, firstfruits, and lunar cycles, not to the names or cults of those deities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=9693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:33</a> | What are your thoughts on James Kugel's *How to Read the Bible*, and is it worth it? | He has heard some good things but has not read it, so he will not recommend it. | He gives a deliberately limited answer: he has heard good things and says it might be worth reading, but he has not read the book himself. Because of that, he does not want to recommend it or offer a firm judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=9725" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:05</a> | What are your thoughts on Dr. Titus Kennedy? | He does not recommend him and says Kennedy plays fast and loose with evidence. | He says he would not recommend Titus Kennedy because he considers Kennedy's materials unreliable. His criticism is that Kennedy is associated with Associates for Biblical Research, favors an early Exodus agenda, and often takes evidence out of historical context to create archaeological proof claims. He also notes Kennedy's involvement with *Patterns of Evidence* as part of why he distrusts the work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=9856" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:16</a> | What are the identities of the nations separated at the Tower of Babel? | Unknown; it is too early before written history to identify the nations securely. | He says the people were in Mesopotamia, but their specific identities cannot be pinned down. He mentions the Eridu expansion hypothesis but rejects it as likely too late, especially given other material cultures such as Nagada in the Nile Valley. Because Babel would be before written history, he says the material culture and ethnic labels are too early and too uncertain to identify securely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=9969" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:09</a> | Is it irrational for lay people to disregard miracles a priori? | Yes, but he also warns against multiplying miracles where the Bible does not claim them. | He says discounting anything a priori is irrational, while a posteriori evaluation based on evidence can be reasonable. He believes miracles are possible in an open universe, but he also warns that some interpreters invoke unnecessary compound miracles, such as elaborate Red Sea crossing scenarios, where the Bible itself does not require them. His rule is to affirm miracles where the Bible presents them as miracles, while allowing God to use natural means too. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=10273" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:13</a> | How would someone construct a good case for young-earth creationism? | It would need to be made on scientific grounds while abandoning weak biblical backdating and hermeneutics. | He says a defensible young-earth case would have to be made almost entirely on scientific grounds, not by using biblical genealogies to backdate the earth or the Flood. He argues that common American young-earth hermeneutics create historical problems, such as placing the Flood after the pyramids. A stronger case would abandon that biblical dating method and then attempt the heavy scientific work of challenging Darwinism or conventional chronology directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=10550" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:50</a> | How misguided is the notion that Egyptians wrote everything down and we have their complete chronological history? | Very misguided; Egyptian records are fragmentary, mostly religious, and many administrative records are lost or poorly preserved. | He says the idea of a complete Egyptian record badly misunderstands Egyptian archaeology. Most surviving writing is religious, funerary, or temple material, while administrative, court, diplomatic, and everyday records are sparse. Memphis and the Delta preserve poorly because groundwater, salt, and papyrus loss destroy records, and even stone can crumble. Chronology is strong for some periods, such as late Dynasty 18 and early Dynasty 19, but weak or uncertain in others, including the Middle Kingdom, Old Kingdom, and Dynasty 13. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=11024" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:44</a> | Were you educated under Alan Millard at Liverpool, and who were your most influential scholars? | No; Millard had retired. His supervisors were Mark Collier and Steven Snape, and Kenneth Kitchen was the strongest influence. | He says Alan Millard had already retired when he was at Liverpool, though he became friends with him through Kenneth Kitchen. His doctoral supervisors were Mark Collier and Steven Snape, while Kitchen was his strongest influence because Falk served as Kitchen's research assistant. He emphasizes Kitchen's breadth as an Egyptologist, historian, epigrapher, and old-style orientalist able to work across Hebrew, Hittite, Akkadian, Greek, Latin, and other ancient Near Eastern materials. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KvObTy1LgnE?t=11474" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:14</a> | What is the historical timeline developed by a computer program that you liked, and where can it be found? | It is his own Groundhog Chronology program, available at `www.groundhogchronology.com`. | He says the program is one he developed himself, drawing on his previous work as a systems analyst and programmer and on the mechanical way Kenneth Kitchen approached chronology. The project began as a Perl proof of concept to test whether a computer could simulate and validate chronological possibilities. He says the site is `www.groundhogchronology.com`, though it still needs more work, more synchronisms, and code refinement. |
